Ayahs

ٱقْتَرَبَتِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ وَٱنشَقَّ ٱلْقَمَرُ (١)
The Hour has drawn near and the moon is split. (1)
وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ ءَايَةً يُعْرِضُوا۟ وَيَقُولُوا۟ سِحْرٌ مُّسْتَمِرٌّ (٢)
If they see a sign, they turn away, and say, ‘An incessant magic!’ (2)
وَكَذَّبُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ (٣)
They denied and followed their own desires, and every matter has a setting [appropriate to it]. (3)

Content of Surah al-Qamar

This Surah possesses the characteristics of Meccan Surahs, which feature intense discussions on the Origin (Monotheism) and the Resurrection. Specifically, it describes the punishments of several past nations who, due to their stubbornness, animosity, and pursuit of the path of disbelief, injustice, and corruption, were afflicted and destroyed one after another by crushing divine torments.

Following each of these accounts, the verse: "وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ" ("And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?") is repeated to serve as a lesson for both Muslims and disbelievers.

Overall, the content of this Surah can be summarized into several sections:

  1. The beginning of the Surah, which speaks of the proximity of the Day of Resurrection, the subject of the "Splitting of the Moon" (Shaqq al-Qamar), and the insistence of the opponents in denying divine signs.

  2. Another section briefly discusses the first rebellious, defiant, and stubborn people—the "People of Noah"—and the event of the Flood.

  3. Another section explains the story of the people of "‘Ad" and their painful punishment.

  4. The fourth section discusses the people of "Thamud," their opposition to their Prophet "Salih" عليه السلام, the "Miracle of the She-Camel," and finally their punishment by the "Heavenly Blast."

  5. It then moves to the people of "Lot" عليه السلام, and while providing a vivid and concise reference to their disbelief and moral deviation, it mentions a portion of their painful torment.

  6. In another section, there is a very brief mention of the "People of Pharaoh" and their punishment.

  7. In the final section, a comparison is made between these nations and the polytheists of Mecca and the opponents of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, recounting the dangerous future they face if they continue on this path. The Surah concludes with a description of some of the punishments for the criminals on the Day of Resurrection and the magnificent rewards for the righteous.

The verses of the Surah are generally short, very intense, and soul-stirring.

The naming of the Surah as "al-Qamar" (The Moon) is due to the first verse of this Surah, which discusses the "Splitting of the Moon."

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

من قرأ سورة اقتربت الساعة فى كل غب بعث يوم القيامة و وجهه على صورة القمر ليلة البدر، و من قرأها كل ليلة كان افضل، و جاء يوم القيامة و وجهه مسفر على وجوه الخلائق:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Iqtarabat al-Sa'ah' every other day will be raised on the Day of Resurrection with his face like the moon on the night of its fullness; and whoever recites it every night, it is better, and he will come on the Day of Resurrection with his face shining brighter than the faces of [other] creatures." [1] Certainly, this radiance of the face on the scene of Resurrection is a sign of strong and true faith achieved under the shadow of reciting this Surah, reflecting upon it, and then acting upon it—not a recitation devoid of thought and action.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, beginning of Surah al-Qamar.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَ انْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ (1) وَ إِنْ يَرَوْا آيَةً يُعْرِضُوا وَ يَقُولُوا سِحْرٌ مُسْتَمِرٌّ (2) وَ كَذَّبُوا وَ اتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ وَ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُسْتَقِرٌّ (3)

Commentary

The Moon Was Split!

In the first verse, two important events are mentioned: one is the approach of the Day of Resurrection, which brings with it the greatest transformation in the world of creation and is a beginning for a new life in another world—a world whose greatness and vastness are beyond the comprehension and description of us, the prisoners of this world.

The other event is the great miracle of the Splitting of the Moon, which is both a proof of the power of the Great Allah over all things and a sign of the truthfulness of the call of His Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

He says: "The Hour has come near, and the moon has split!" (اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَ انْشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ).

It is noteworthy that the previous Surah (Surah al-Najm) ended with phrases regarding the proximity of the Resurrection: "The Approaching Day has approached" (azifat al-azifah), and this Surah begins with the same meaning. This is an emphasis on the fact that the Resurrection is near, although on the scale of the world's lifespan it may take thousands of years. However, considering the total lifespan of this world on one hand, and the fact that the entire life of the world is nothing more than a passing moment compared to the Resurrection, the meaning of this expression becomes clear.

The mention of these two events together, as a group of commentators have stated, is because the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله—who is the last of the divine prophets—is itself one of the signs of the proximity of the Resurrection. Therefore, in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, we read that he said:

بعثت انا و الساعة كهاتين‌

"I and the Hour were sent like these two" [1] (pointing to his two blessed fingers placed next to each other).

On the other hand, the splitting of the moon is itself a proof of the possibility of the disruption of the system of the celestial bodies and a small example of the great events that will occur in this world on the threshold of the Resurrection, for all the planets, stars, and the earth will collapse, and a new world will be created in their place.

According to famous narrations, some of which are claimed to be mutawatir (frequently reported), the polytheists came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "If you are telling the truth and you are the Prophet of Allah, split the moon for us into two pieces!" He said: "If I do this, will you believe?" They said, "Yes"—and that night was the fourteenth night of the month. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله requested from the presence of His Lord to grant him what they had asked. Suddenly, the moon split into two pieces, and the Messenger of Allah called them one by one, saying: "Look!" [1]

As for how it is possible for this massive celestial sphere to be split, and what effects such an event would have on the Earth and the solar system? And the manner of the attraction of the two halves of the moon after splitting, and how it is possible for such an event to have occurred without world histories mentioning it? And other questions in this regard—God willing, we will discuss them in detail in the "Key Points" section.

A point necessary to mention here is that some commentators, influenced by certain negative suggestions and having denied any supernatural act (except the Quran) for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, have found it difficult—given the clarity of the above verse and the abundance of narrations cited in the books of Islamic scholars—to justify this miracle and bypass it in a way that denies its miraculous nature.

However, the truth is that the issue of "Shaqq al-Qamar" was performed as a miracle, and the subsequent verses contain clear evidence of this. It would have been better for them to reconsider that incorrect belief so they might know that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله also had miracles and supernatural acts. If some verses of the Quran deny them, they refer to "demanded miracles" (mu'jizat iqtirahi) that a group of excuse-seekers proposed, having no intention of accepting the truth nor submitting to it after its performance. However, miracles requested for the investigation of the truth were performed by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and there are numerous evidences for this in the history of his life.

Then the Quran adds: "When the stubborn opponents see a sign or a miracle proving the truth of your call, they turn away and say, 'This is a continuous magic!'" (وَ إِنْ يَرَوْا آيَةً يُعْرِضُوا وَ يَقُولُوا سِحْرٌ مُسْتَمِرٌّ).

The expression "continuous" (mustamirr) refers to the fact that they had seen repeated miracles from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, of which the splitting of the moon was a continuation. They attributed them all to the persistence of magic and perceived it as "continuous magic," even though this accusation was merely an excuse for not submitting to the truth.

Some commentators have interpreted mustamirr to mean "powerful" (as they say hablun marir, meaning a strong rope), and some have interpreted it as "passing and unstable," but the first interpretation is the most apparent.

In the next verse, it points to the reason for their opposition, as well as the sinister result of this opposition, adding: "They denied and followed their own desires, and every matter has a place of settlement" (وَ كَذَّبُوا وَ اتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ وَ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُسْتَقِرٌّ).

The source of their opposition and denial of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, or the denial of his miracles and proofs, as well as the denial of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, was following their whims and desires. Prejudices, stubbornness, and selfishness did not allow them to submit to the truth. On the other hand, the desire for lack of restraint to indulge in pleasures without any conditions, and the indulgence in every sin and injustice, prevented them from accepting the call of truth, because accepting this call brought responsibility.

Yes, it has always been so, and it will always be so, that the great obstacle in the path of truth is "worship of desires."

The meaning of the phrase "وَ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُسْتَقِرٌّ" ("and every matter has a place of settlement") is that everyone will reach the recompense of their deeds; the settlement of good is for the good, and the settlement of evil is for the evil.

This expression likely refers to the fact that nothing in this world perishes, and every good and bad deed remains fixed and enduring until man sees its reward.

Another possibility in the interpretation of the above verse is that denials and accusations cannot hide the face of truth forever; rather, everything moves toward its own settlement, and it will not be long before the beautiful face of truth is revealed, and the ugly and detestable face of falsehood also appears. This is a divine law in the universe.

These interpretations are not contradictory, and it is possible that all of them are included in the concept of the verse.

Key Points

1. The Splitting of the Moon: A Great Miracle of the Prophet of Islam (ص)

Although some narrow-minded individuals insist on justifying this miracle in a way that removes it from being a supernatural event—such as saying the above verse informs of the future and relates to the "Signs of the Hour," i.e., events before the Resurrection—there are several clues in the verse that emphasize the occurrence of a miracle. Among them is the mention of this subject in the "past tense," which shows that "Shaqq al-Qamar" has occurred, just as the proximity of the Resurrection with the appearance of the last Prophet has also been realized.

Furthermore, if it were not a matter of a miracle, it would have no relevance to the attribution of magic to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله mentioned in the next verse, nor would it be consistent with the phrase "they denied and followed their desires," which reports their denial.

In addition to these, numerous narrations have been reported in Islamic books regarding the occurrence of this miracle, reaching the level of fame or tawatur, and thus it cannot be denied. Here we refer to the statements of "Fakhr al-Razi" and "Tabarsi," two famous commentators of the Sunni and Shi'a schools. Fakhr al-Razi says: "The majority of commentators believe the meaning of the verse is that the moon was split, and authentic narrations also indicate this meaning. Its rational possibility is also beyond doubt. On the other hand, the Truthful Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله informed of it; therefore, it must be accepted. As for the story of the impossibility of 'tearing and mending' (kharq wa iltiyam) in the celestial spheres (according to the debunked Ptolemaic view), it is a baseless and unscientific claim, for it has been proven by rational proofs that tearing and destruction in the heavens are entirely possible."

The late Tabarsi also says in Majma' al-Bayan: "Commentators consider this verse to be related to the miracle of the Splitting of the Moon during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله." He then mentions only 'Ata, Hasan, and Balkhi as those who held the opposite view, dismissing their opinion with indifference.

Some have reported that "Hudhayfah," the famous companion, recounted the story of the Splitting of the Moon in the presence of a large gathering in the mosque of Mada'in, and no one objected to him, even though many of those present had lived during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (this Hadith is cited in al-Durr al-Manthur and by al-Qurtubi under the verse in question).

Therefore, neither considering the verse itself and the existing clues within it, nor in terms of narrations and the sayings of commentators, is the subject of the Splitting of the Moon deniable. Of course, there are questions here that we will address.

2. The Splitting of the Moon from the Perspective of Modern Science

One of the important questions raised in this discussion is: Is the occurrence of splitting and cracking in celestial bodies fundamentally possible? Or does science completely deny it?

The answer to this question, considering the studies and discoveries of astronomers, is not very complex. Discoveries show that such a thing is not only not impossible but has been observed many times, although different factors were effective in each case.

In other words: splits and explosions have repeatedly occurred in the solar system and other celestial bodies. For example, we recall the following cases:

A. The Origin of the Solar System

This theory is accepted by all scientists: that all the planets of the solar system were originally part of the sun, which later separated from it and each began to rotate in its own orbit.

However, there is debate regarding the cause of this separation:

"Laplace" believed the cause of this separation was "centrifugal force" in the equatorial region of the sun. This means that when the sun was a mass of burning gas (as it still is) and was rotating around itself, the speed of this rotation in the equatorial region caused pieces to separate from it, scatter in space, and rotate around the main center, the sun.

But the research of some other scientists after Laplace led to another hypothesis, which considers the cause of this separation to be the occurrence of intense tides on the surface of the sun due to the passage of a massive star near it.

Proponents of this hypothesis, who do not consider the sun's rotation at that time sufficient to justify the separation of pieces from it, have turned to this hypothesis, saying: these tides created massive waves on the surface of the sun, just like the fall of a massive stone into an ocean, and as a result, pieces of the sun were thrown out one after another and began to rotate around the mother sphere.

In any case, whatever the cause of separation, it does not prevent everyone from believing that the origin of the solar system occurred through splitting and separations.

B. "Asteroids"

"Asteroids" are pieces of massive celestial rocks that rotate around the solar system, and they are sometimes referred to as small planets or "protoplanets." Some are so large that their diameter reaches 25 kilometers, but they are usually smaller than this.

Scientists believe asteroids are the remains of a massive planet that was moving in an orbit between the orbit of "Mars" and the orbit of "Jupiter," then exploded and split due to unknown factors.

So far, more than 5,000 asteroids have been discovered and observed, and many of the larger ones have been named, and their volume, mass, and duration of rotation around the sun have been calculated. Some astronomers attribute special importance to asteroids and believe they can occasionally be used as bases for travel to distant points in space! This is another example of splitting in celestial bodies.

C. "Meteors"

"Meteors" are very small celestial rocks that sometimes do not exceed the size of a hazelnut, and in any case, they rotate around the sun at high speeds in specific orbits. Sometimes, when their path intersects with the Earth's orbit, they are attracted toward the Earth.

These small rocks, due to the intensity of the collision with the air surrounding the Earth and because of their staggering speed, become hot, glowing, and ignited, and we observe them as a beautiful line of light within the Earth's atmosphere, referring to them as "shooting stars."

Sometimes we imagine it is a distant star falling, while it is a small meteor that caught fire at a very close distance and then turned to ash.

The orbit of meteors contacts the Earth's orbit at two points; for this reason, in the months of Mordad (July/August) and Aban (October/November), which are the two intersection points of the two orbits, meteors are seen more frequently.

Scientists say: these are the remains of a comet that exploded and split apart due to unknown events.

This is yet another example of splitting in celestial spheres.

In any case, the issue of explosion and splitting in celestial spheres is not unprecedented and is never impossible from a scientific point of view such that it could be said that miracles do not apply to the impossible.

These are all regarding splitting, but its return to its normal state due to the gravitational force existing between the two pieces is entirely possible.

Although in the old astronomy, which revolved around the "Ptolemaic" view and its nine onion-skin-like spheres, these crystalline spheres were so interconnected that their "tearing and mending" was considered impossible by some. Therefore, followers of this view denied both the physical Ascension (Mi'raj) and the Splitting of the Moon, because they necessitated splitting and then mending in the spheres. But today, when the Ptolemaic hypothesis has been consigned to the realm of myths and imaginary legends, and no trace of those nine spheres remains, there is no basis for such talk.

Perhaps this point does not need reminding: the Splitting of the Moon did not occur under an ordinary natural factor, but rather it had a miraculous aspect. However, since miracles do not apply to the impossible, the intention here was to state the possibility of this matter (take note).

3. The Splitting of the Moon from a Historical Perspective

Another objection that some uninformed people have regarding the "Splitting of the Moon" is that if this matter were a reality, with all its importance, it should have been recorded in world histories, whereas it is not.

But to clarify to what extent this objection is significant, an analysis must be conducted on various aspects of this issue:

A. It must be noted that the moon is always visible in half of the Earth, not in all of it; therefore, half of the people on Earth are excluded from this count.

B. In half of this same hemisphere, the vast majority of people are asleep, as it is after midnight; thus, only one-fourth of the world's people could potentially be aware of such an event.

C. In the visible section, there is no obstacle to significant parts of the sky being cloudy and the face of the moon being covered by clouds.

D. Celestial events attract people's attention only if they are either accompanied by loud noises like thunderclaps or if the light is completely cut off for a relatively long period, like total lunar and solar eclipses.

For this reason, in partial eclipses and minor lunar darkenings, if they are not previously announced by astronomers, few people become aware, and sometimes many people remain unaware even of a total eclipse.

Only scientists who observe celestial bodies and the moon, or those whose eyes happen to fall upon the sky, might become aware of such a process and inform others.

Therefore, such a short-lived event is not as much a cause for general public attention worldwide as initially imagined, especially for the people of that time who generally attributed less importance to celestial bodies.

E. Furthermore, the means of recording information in history and its dissemination were limited at that time; even the number of literate people was very small, and books were handwritten. It was not like today, where important events are published lightning-fast via radio, television, and the press throughout the world.

Given these and other factors, one can never be surprised by the lack of mention of this event in non-Islamic histories, nor take it as proof of its denial.

4. The Date of the Occurrence of This Great Miracle

There is no debate among commentators and narrators of Hadith that the Splitting of the Moon occurred in Mecca and before the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration (Hijrah). However, it is understood from some narrations that this matter was at the beginning of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission [1], while from others it appears that this event occurred near the Hijrah and in the late Meccan period. This was also at the request of truth-seekers who came from Medina to see the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and pledged allegiance to him at 'Aqabah [2]. Also, in some narrations, we read that the reason for proposing the "Splitting of the Moon" to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was that they said magic affects earthly matters, and we want to be sure that Muhammad's صلى الله عليه وآله miracles are not magic [3].

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 17, p. 354 (Hadith 8).

[2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 17, p. 352 (Hadith 1).

[3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 17, p. 355 (Hadith 10).

Even a group of stubborn fanatics, after seeing this great miracle, said: "We will not accept until the caravans of Syria and Yemen arrive, and we ask them: Did they see such a thing on their path?" But when the travelers said, "We saw it," they still did not believe! [1]

The final point necessary to mention here is that: this great miracle, like many other miracles, has been mixed with superstitions in weak histories and narrations that have distorted its image in the eyes of thinkers—such as a piece of the moon falling to the earth. However, these superstitions must be carefully separated from it so that the original face of the miracle, which has a scientific aspect, is not tarnished.

[1]: al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 133.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَنۢبَآءِ مَا فِيهِ مُزْدَجَرٌ (٤)
There have already come to them reports containing admonishment, (4)
حِكْمَةٌۢ بَـٰلِغَةٌ ۖ فَمَا تُغْنِ ٱلنُّذُرُ (٥)
[and representing] far-reaching wisdom; but warnings are of no avail! (5)
فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ۘ يَوْمَ يَدْعُ ٱلدَّاعِ إِلَىٰ شَىْءٍ نُّكُرٍ (٦)
So turn away from them! The day when the Caller calls to a dire thing, (6)
خُشَّعًا أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرَادٌ مُّنتَشِرٌ (٧)
they will emerge from the graves as if they were scattered locusts with a humbled look [in their eyes], (7)
مُّهْطِعِينَ إِلَى ٱلدَّاعِ ۖ يَقُولُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَسِرٌ (٨)
scrambling toward the summoner. The faithless will say, ‘This is a hard day!’ (8)

Commentary

The Day Everyone Emerges from the Graves

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding a group of disbelievers who denied the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and would not submit to any miracle, the verses under discussion provide further detail about such individuals and their painful fate on the Day of Resurrection.

First, It states: It is not as if this group is uninformed; rather, "The news that could cause them to feel aversion toward ugliness and evil has come to them in sufficient measure" (وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْبَاءِ مَا فِيهِ مُزْدَجَرٌ).

Therefore, there has been no deficiency in the propagation of the Divine callers; "whatever [fault] there is, it is due to their own unharmonious and ill-proportioned stature." They possess neither a listening ear, nor a truth-seeking spirit, nor even that minimum amount of piety that would invite them to investigate and contemplate the Divine verses.

The meaning of "Anba" (news/reports) refers to the accounts of previous nations and peoples who were destroyed by various punishments, as well as the news of the Resurrection and the punishment of the oppressors and disbelievers, which the Quran has unveiled.

Then It adds: "These verses are a profound Divine wisdom and deep, eloquent warnings; however, these warnings are not beneficial to these stubborn individuals and do not profit them" (حِكْمَةٌ بَالِغَةٌ فَمَا تُغْنِ النُّذُرُ) [1] [2]

[1]: "حِكْمَةٌ بَالِغَةٌ" is the predicate (khabar) of an omitted subject (mubtada), and the implied meaning is "هذه حكمة بالغة" (This is a profound wisdom).

[2]: "نذر" (Nudhur) is the plural of "Nadhir" (meaning warners), encompassing Divine verses, the reports of previous nations, and the Prophets whose voices reached the people. Some have also suggested that "Nudhur" is a verbal noun meaning "warning," but the first meaning is more appropriate. Additionally, the "ma" in "فما تغن النذر" is negative, not interrogative.

In summary, there is no defect in the "agency of the Agent"; whatever [defect] exists is in the "receptivity of the recipient." Otherwise, the Divine verses, the great Prophets, the reports received from previous nations, and the news given to them about the Resurrection are each a profound and eloquent wisdom that could impact their souls and spirits if even the slightest spiritual readiness existed.

In the next verse, It says: "Now that these strangers to the truth have absolutely no readiness for acceptance, leave them to themselves, turn away from them, and seek out the ready hearts" (فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ).

"And remember the day when the Divine caller invites the people to a terrifying thing"—an invitation to the accounting and the examination of the records of deeds (يَوْمَ يَدْعُ الدَّاعِ إِلَى شَيْءٍ نُكُرٍ) [1]. Therefore, the phrase "يَوْمَ يَدْعُ الدَّاعِ" is an independent sentence separate from the phrase "فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ," although some have considered it a continuation of the previous phrase, suggesting the meaning is: "On the Day of Resurrection when the Divine caller calls and they cling to you for intercession, turn away from them." However, this interpretation seems very far-fetched.

Is this "caller" Allah? Or His angels? Or Israfil, who calls the people to the Resurrection by blowing the Trumpet? Or is it all of them? Commentators have offered various possibilities.

[1]: "يوم" (The Day) in the above verse relates to an omitted "اذكر" (Remember), and some have suggested it relates to "يخرجون" (they emerge), but this seems unlikely.

However, considering verse 52 of Surah Al-Isra, which states: يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ ("Remember the day He calls you [from your graves] and you respond by praising Him"), the first meaning is more appropriate, although the upcoming verses are more consistent with the meaning that the angels and the officials of accounting and retribution are intended.

The meaning of "Shay’in Nukur" (an unknown/horrific thing) is either the precise Divine accounting which was unknown to them until that day, or unknown punishments that they never believed in, or all of these, because the Resurrection, in all its standards, is unknown to human beings [1].

In the next verse, providing more explanation in this regard, It says: "They emerge from the graves while their eyes are cast down in intense terror, running in every direction without a goal, like scattered locusts!" (خُشَّعاً أَبْصارُهُمْ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرادٌ مُنْتَشِرٌ). The attribution of "humility" (khushu') to the "eyes" is because the scene is so horrific that they cannot bear to look at it; thus, they avert their eyes and cast them downward.

The comparison to "scattered locusts" is appropriate because swarms of locusts—unlike many birds that move with a specific order and arrangement during collective movement—never have order or arrangement; they crash into one another and move aimlessly in every direction. Furthermore, on that day, they are weak and helpless creatures like locusts.

Yes, these ignorant, blind-hearted ones will be so terror-stricken on that day that, like drunken people, they will turn in every direction without awareness and collide with one another, as if they have lost their senses, just as we read in verse 2 of Surah Al-Hajj: وَتَرَى النَّاسَ سُكَارَى وَمَا هُمْ بِسُكَارَى ("And you see the people as if they are drunk, but they are not drunk").

In reality, this comparison is similar to what is mentioned in verse 4 of Surah Al-Qari'ah: يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَرَاشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ ("Remember the day when people will be like scattered moths").

Then It adds: "When they emerge from the graves following this call, they stretch their necks toward the calling angels out of intense terror" (مُهْطِعِينَ إِلَى الدَّاعِ). "Muhti'in" is from the root "Ihta'," meaning to stretch the neck, and some have interpreted it as staring fixedly or running swiftly toward something. Each of these meanings is possible in the interpretation of the verse, though the first meaning seems more appropriate, because when a person hears a terrifying sound, they immediately stretch their neck and turn toward the source of the sound. Of course, there is no obstacle to all these meanings being combined in the concept of the verse: that upon hearing the voice of the Divine caller, they stretch their necks toward Him, then stare fixedly, and then go toward Him with speed and appear in the Divine court.

It is here that terror from the harsh events of that day envelops their entire being; thus, in the continuation of the verse, It adds: "The disbelievers say: This is a difficult and painful day!" (يَقُولُ الْكَافِرُونَ هَذَا يَوْمٌ عَسِرٌ).

And truly, it is a difficult day, for Allah has also confirmed this meaning, stating in verse 26 of Surah Al-Furqan: وَكَانَ يَوْماً عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ عَسِيراً ("And it is a day difficult for the disbelievers").

However, it is inferred from this expression that the day is not a difficult one for the believers!

Key Points Why is the Resurrection an extremely difficult day?

Why should it not be a difficult day? While all factors of fear and terror surround the criminals.

On one hand, when the record of their deeds is placed in their hands, their cry rises: يَا وَيْلَتَنَا مَالِ هَذَا الْكِتَابِ لَا يُغَادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصَاهَا ("Woe to us! What is this book that leaves out nothing small or large except that it has accounted for it?") (Kahf: 49).

On the other hand, if they have performed the smallest good or evil deed, the account of all of them is examined with ultimate precision: إِنْ تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُنْ فِي صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ أَوْ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ ("If there be [a deed] the weight of a mustard seed, even if it be within a rock or in the heavens or in the earth, Allah will bring it forth [for accounting]. Indeed, Allah is Subtle and Aware") (Luqman: 16).

On the third hand, there is no way to compensate there, no excuse is heard, and the path of return is absolutely closed, as the Quran says: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْماً لَا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَنْ نَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَاعَةٌ وَلَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ ("And fear a day when no soul will suffice for another soul at all, nor will intercession be accepted from it, nor will compensation be taken from it, nor will they be helped") (Baqarah: 48).

And we also read: وَلَوْ تَرَى إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى النَّارِ فَقَالُوا يَا لَيْتَنَا نُرَدُّ وَلَا نُكَذِّبَ بِآيَاتِ رَبِّنَا وَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ ("If you could but see when they are made to stand before the Fire and they say, 'Oh, would that we could be returned [to the world] and not deny the signs of our Lord and be among the believers!'") (But it will not be accepted from them) (An'am: 27).

And on the fourth hand, the Divine punishment is so severe that mothers forget their children, pregnant women miscarry, and people appear dazed and drunk while they are not drunk, but the punishment of Allah is severe: يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَهَا تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّا أَرْضَعَتْ وَتَضَعُ كُلُّ ذَاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَهَا وَتَرَى النَّاسَ سُكَارَى وَمَا هُمْ بِسُكَارَى وَلَكِنَّ عَذَابَ اللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ (Hajj: 2).

For this reason, "the sinners become so afflicted with anxiety and terror that they wish to give everything in the world to be saved from the Divine punishment": يَوَدُّ الْمُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدِي مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِئِذٍ بِبَنِيهِ وَصَاحِبَتِهِ وَأَخِيهِ وَفَصِيلَتِهِ الَّتِي تُؤْوِيهِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ يُنْجِيهِ كَلَّا إِنَّهَا لَظَى ("The criminal will wish that he could ransom himself from the punishment of that day by his children, and his wife and his brother, and his nearest kindred who sheltered him, and whoever is on earth entirely, then [it] might save him. No! Indeed, it is the Flame [of Hell]") (Ma'arij: 11-15).

With these descriptions and other soul-shaking descriptions reflected in other verses of the Quran, is it possible for that day not to be a difficult, painful, and sorrowful day?

(May Allah protect us all under the shelter of His grace on that day).

Ayahs

۞ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ فَكَذَّبُوا۟ عَبْدَنَا وَقَالُوا۟ مَجْنُونٌ وَٱزْدُجِرَ (٩)
The people of Noah impugned before them. So they impugned Our servant and said, ‘A crazy man,’ and he was reviled. (9)
فَدَعَا رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنِّى مَغْلُوبٌ فَٱنتَصِرْ (١٠)
Thereat he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘I have been overcome, so help [me].’ (10)
فَفَتَحْنَآ أَبْوَٰبَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَآءٍ مُّنْهَمِرٍ (١١)
Then We opened the gates of the sky with pouring waters, (11)
وَفَجَّرْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا فَٱلْتَقَى ٱلْمَآءُ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ (١٢)
and We made the earth burst forth with springs, and the waters met for a preordained purpose. (12)
وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْوَٰحٍ وَدُسُرٍ (١٣)
We bore him on a vessel made of planks and nails, (13)
تَجْرِى بِأَعْيُنِنَا جَزَآءً لِّمَن كَانَ كُفِرَ (١٤)
which sailed [over the flood waters] in Our sight, as a retribution for him who was met with disbelief. (14)
وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَـٰهَآ ءَايَةً فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (١٥)
Certainly We have left it as a sign; so is there anyone who will be admonished? (15)
فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (١٦)
So how were My punishment and warnings? (16)
وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (١٧)
Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonishment. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (17)

Commentary

The Story of the People of Noah was a Lesson of Admonition

It is the Sunnah (custom) of the Quran that in many instances, after warning the disbelievers and criminals, it provides an account of the history of previous nations and their painful end. This is to make them understand that if they continue their incorrect path, they will face a fate no better than theirs.

In this Surah as well, following the discussion in the previous verses, short yet meaningful references are made to the history of five rebellious nations from the past, the first of whom is the people of Noah عليه السلام. It says:

"Before them, the people of Noah denied [their Prophet]" (كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ‌).

"Yes, they denied Our servant Noah and said: 'He is a madman!' and then, through various forms of harassment and torture, they prevented him from continuing his mission" (فَكَذَّبُوا عَبْدَنا وَ قالُوا مَجْنُونٌ وَ ازْدُجِرَ).

Sometimes "they would say to him: 'If you do not desist, O Noah, you will surely be among those stoned'" (قَالُوا لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ يَا نُوحُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمَرْجُومِينَ‌) (Ash-Shu'ara 116).

And sometimes they would squeeze his throat so hard that he would fall to the ground unconscious; however, when he regained consciousness, he would say: "O Allah, forgive my people, for they do not know!" [^1] In short, they harassed him in every way they could, yet he did not cease his preaching and guidance.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, "denial" (تكذيب) is mentioned twice; apparently, because the first time it is mentioned generally, and the second time is its explanation.

The expression "Our servant" (عَبْدَنا) indicates that these arrogant and rebellious people were, in reality, opposing Us, not just the person of Noah! The phrase "وَ ازْدُجِرَ" is originally from "zajr," meaning to drive away or repel someone with a loud voice and shouting, but it is applied to any action taken to prevent someone from continuing a task. Interestingly, in the verse under discussion, "قالوا" (they said) is in the active voice, while "وَ ازْدُجِرَ" (and he was rebuked/driven away) is in the passive voice. Perhaps this is because their actions in rebuking Noah were worse and more improper than their words, to the extent that Allah does not even name this group and does not consider them worthy of mention.

Then it adds: "When Noah became completely despaired of their guidance, he appealed to the Divine threshold: 'O my Lord! This rebellious and criminal group has overcome me, so take my revenge from them'" (فَدَعا رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ).

They never overcame me in terms of evidence, proof, or logic, but they overcame me through oppression, crime, denial, rejection, and various forms of rebuke and pressure. This nation is no longer worthy of survival; take revenge upon them and grant me victory over them. [^1] Indeed, this great Prophet, as long as he had hope for their guidance, would ask Allah to forgive them, but when he became completely despaired, he prayed against them.

Then, it makes an eloquent and soul-shaking reference to the nature of their punishment, saying: "Following this request of Noah, We opened the gates of heaven, and a heavy and continuous rain poured down" (فَفَتَحْنا أَبْوابَ السَّماءِ بِماءٍ مُنْهَمِرٍ).

The expression "opening the gates of heaven" is a very beautiful metaphor used for the falling of torrential rains, just as we say in Persian: "It is as if the gates of heaven have opened and all the water is pouring down."

"Munhamir" is from the root "hamr" (on the pattern of 'sabr'), meaning the heavy pouring of tears or water. This expression is also used for milking an animal's udder to the very last drop.

Surprisingly, it is mentioned in some commentaries that they had been suffering from drought for years and were waiting for rain, when suddenly a rain poured down—but a rain of death, not of life. [^1]

Not only did a vast amount of water pour down from the sky, but water also gushed from the earth, as stated in the verse: "And We caused the earth to burst with springs" (وَ فَجَّرْنَا الْأَرْضَ عُيُوناً) ^2 "And these two waters met for a purpose already predestined" and covered the entire earth! (فَالْتَقَى الْماءُ عَلى‌ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ). Some commentators have interpreted the phrase "قَدْ قُدِرَ" (already predestined) to mean that these two waters were exactly equal in volume, but the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In summary, water began to gush from the entire earth and springs emerged, and water began to pour from the entire sky, and they joined together to form a vast and stormy sea.

At this point, the Quran leaves the rest of the flood story (since what needed to be said is contained in the previous sentences) and turns to Noah's ark of salvation, saying: "We carried Noah on a vessel made of 'planks' and 'nails'" (وَ حَمَلْناهُ عَلى‌ ذاتِ أَلْواحٍ وَ دُسُرٍ).

"Dusur" is the plural of "disar" (on the pattern of 'kitab'). As Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, it originally means driving something forcefully and with power. Since a nail is driven into wood and the like with forceful blows, it is called "disar." However, some commentators have interpreted this word to mean "ropes," referring to the ropes of the ship's sails and such, but the first meaning seems more correct, especially considering the mention of "planks" (alwah).

In any case, the Quranic expression here is interesting and meaningful, for it says that in the midst of that massive and global storm that swallowed everything, We entrusted the command of saving Noah and his companions to a handful of nails and pieces of wood, and they performed this duty well! This is a great manifestation of power.

This expression might also implicitly refer to the simplicity of the ships of that time compared to the advanced ships of later eras, which did not have specific formalities or structures at that time. Nevertheless, Noah's ark was large enough, and according to history, he worked for years to build it so that he could house a pair of every different animal in it.

Then Allah refers to His special grace toward Noah's ark of salvation, saying: "This ship split the chest of the waves before Our eyes (knowledge) and continued its movement under Our supervision and protection" (تَجْرِي بِأَعْيُنِنا). The expression "بِأَعْيُنِنا" (before Our eyes) is a subtle metaphor for special attention and complete care for something, just as we read in verse 37 of Surah Hud in another part of this same story: وَ اصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا وَ وَحْيِنا: "And construct the ship before Our eyes and according to Our revelation."

Some commentators have considered this expression to refer to the distinguished individuals who were aboard the ship. [^1] Therefore, the sentence "تَجْرِي بِأَعْيُنِنا" would mean that the ship was carrying Our pure and sincere servants. However, considering the usage of this expression in other verses of the Quran, the first interpretation seems correct.

The possibility has also been raised that "بِأَعْيُنِنا" refers to the angels who were involved in guiding Noah's ship, but this interpretation is also weak for the same reason mentioned above.

Then He adds: "All of this was a recompense for those who denied him and disbelieved" (جَزاءً لِمَنْ كانَ كُفِرَ). ^2 Yes, Noah, like all prophets, was among the great Divine gifts and His immense blessings, which the ignorant ungratefully rejected and disbelieved in his religion. [^3]

Then, as a conclusion to this great event, He says: "We have left this story as a lesson and a sign among the nations; is there then any who will take heed and remember?" (وَ لَقَدْ تَرَكْناها آيَةً فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ). And truly, everything that needed to be said has been said in this event, and whatever an awakened human should understand, they understand from it.

According to this interpretation, which aligns with the preceding and following verses, the pronoun in "تَرَكْناها" (We left it) refers to the story of the flood and the history of Noah and his opponents. However, some consider it a reference to "Noah's Ark," because this ship remained among the people of the world for a time, and whoever saw it would have the entire story of Noah's flood visualized before them. This is especially true if we accept the narration that the remains of this ship existed until the era of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and some even claim that in our era, its remains have been observed in the Ararat mountains of the Caucasus. It is also possible that the verse refers to both meanings: both the story of Noah عليه السلام was a sign, and his remaining ship among the people. [^1]

In the next verse, as a threatening and meaningful question to the disbelievers who follow the same path as the disbelievers of Noah's time, He says: "Now (say), how was My punishment and My warnings?" (فَكَيْفَ كانَ عَذابِي وَ نُذُرِ). Was it a reality or a story and a legend?!

In the final verse under discussion, He emphasizes this truth: "And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will take heed?" (وَ لَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ). Yes, this Quran has no complexity, and the conditions for influence are gathered within it. Its words are sweet and attractive, its expressions are vivid and meaningful, its warnings and glad tidings are explicit and eloquent, its stories are real and content-rich, its proofs are strong and firm, and its logic is eloquent and solid. In short, whatever is necessary for a speech to be influential is gathered in it. For this reason, whenever ready hearts come into contact with it, they are attracted to it. Throughout the history of Islam, strange and amazing examples of the deep influence of the Quran on ready hearts have been seen, which serve as a living witness to this matter.

But what can be done when the vital germ of a seed is dead? Even if it is placed in the best of lands and irrigated with the water of Kauthar under the supervision of the best gardeners, it will never grow, and no flower or plant will sprout from it.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kashshaf and Abu al-Futuh al-Razi under the verses discussed. [^1]: "Intasara" means to seek help (as in verse 41 of Surah Ash-Shura), but here it is interpreted as taking revenge—a revenge based on justice and wisdom. Some have also said that "intasir li" was implied (meaning "help me"). [^1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verses discussed.

[^1]: "A'yun" is the plural of "ayn," which has several meanings, one of which is "eye" and another is "a person of distinction"; of course, it has other meanings as well. ^2: It should be noted that here "kufira" is in the passive voice and refers to Noah, who was disbelieved in, rather than being an active verb referring to the disbelievers. [^3]: If nothing is assumed to be implied in the verse, the pro-agent (na'ib fa'il) of "kufira" would be Noah himself, who was a blessing that was ungratefully rejected. If we say it was "kufira bihi," it refers to the lack of faith in Noah and his teachings. [^1]: Regarding the story of the people of Noah عليه السلام, we have provided detailed discussions under verses 25 to 49 of Surah Hud (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 9), pages 68 to 125.

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (١٨)
[The people of] ‘Ad impugned [their apostle]. So how were My punishment and warnings? (18)
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِى يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُّسْتَمِرٍّ (١٩)
Indeed We unleashed upon them an icy gale on an incessantly ill-fated day, (19)
تَنزِعُ ٱلنَّاسَ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ مُّنقَعِرٍ (٢٠)
knocking down people as if they were trunks of uprooted palm trees. (20)
فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (٢١)
So how were My punishment and warnings?! (21)
وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (٢٢)
Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonishment. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (22)

Commentary

And Likewise the Fate of the People of ‘Ad

Another people whose story follows the condensed account of the people of Nuh عليه السلام in this Surah is the "People of ‘Ad." The Quran mentions them briefly in the verses under discussion as a warning to the disbelievers and criminals, stating:

"The ‘Ad also denied [their Prophet]" (كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ).

No matter how much their Prophet, Hud عليه السلام, increased his preaching and strove through various means to awaken them, they only increased in their obstinacy and stubbornness. The arrogance stemming from wealth and material resources, and the heedlessness caused by being drowned in desires, had deprived them of a listening ear and a seeing eye.

Ultimately, Allah punished them with a painful torment; thus, in the continuation of this same verse, He says in a summary fashion: "See then, how was My punishment and My warnings?"

(فَكَيْفَ كانَ عَذابِي وَ نُذُرِ).

Then, in the subsequent verses, He elaborates on this brevity, adding: "Indeed, We sent against them a furious and cold wind on a day of continuous ill-fortune" (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحاً صَرْصَراً فِي يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُسْتَمِرٍّ).

"Sarsar" (صَرْصَر) is derived from the root "Sarr" (صرّ), which originally means to tie or fasten firmly. Its repetition in the word "Sarsar" is for emphasis. Since this wind was both intense and cold, as well as piercing and noisy, this term has been applied to it.

"Nahs" (نَحْس) originally means a deep redness that sometimes appears on the horizon, resembling a smokeless flame of fire which the Arabs call "Nuhas" (نُحاس). Subsequently, this word was applied to anything "ominous" or "unlucky" as opposed to "Sa‘d" (auspicious) [Note this carefully].

"Mustamir" (مُسْتَمِرّ - continuous) is an adjective for either "Yawm" (day) or "Nahs" (ill-fortune). In the first case, its meaning is that the events of that day continued unabated, as stated in verse 7 of Surah Al-Haqqah: "He imposed it upon them for seven nights and eight days in succession," until it crushed them all and left no one alive.

In the second case, its meaning is that the ill-fortune of this day continued until it destroyed them all.

Some have also interpreted "Nahs" to mean "dust-laden," because this gale was so filled with dust that they could not see one another. When it appeared from afar, they imagined a rain-heavy cloud was coming toward them! But they soon realized it was a gale commissioned for their punishment and destruction, as stated in verse 24 of Surah Al-Ahqaf: "Then, when they saw it as a cloud advancing toward their valleys, they said, 'This is a cloud bringing us rain!' Nay, it is that which you were seeking to hasten—a wind wherein is a painful torment."

These two interpretations are not contradictory and both may be encompassed within the meaning of the verse.

Then, in describing this gale, He says: "Plucking up men as if they were uprooted trunks of palm trees" and tossing them in every direction (تَنْزِعُ النَّاسَ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ مُنْقَعِرٍ).

"Munqa‘ir" (مُنْقَعِر) is from the root "Qa‘r" (قعر), meaning the lowest point of something; hence, this word is used in the sense of uprooting.

This expression is either because the people of ‘Ad had powerful bodies and large statures, and as some commentators have said, they had built trenches and underground shelters to protect themselves from the gale. However, the power of the gale on that day was such that it uprooted them from their shelters and tossed them hither and thither! It is even said that it slammed them headfirst into the ground so violently that their heads were severed from their bodies! The term "A‘jaz" (أَعْجاز) is the plural of "‘Ajz" (عجز), meaning the rear or lower part of something. Their comparison to the lower part of palm trees is because, as some say, the wind was so fierce that it first tore off their hands and heads and carried them away, and then the rest of their bodies, like palm trunks without branches or leaves, were uprooted from the earth and tossed into every corner.

Or it is because of what we mentioned above: that the wind slammed them headfirst into the ground, breaking necks and severing heads!

Then the Quran says as a warning: "Now see, how was My punishment and My warnings?" (فَكَيْفَ كانَ عَذابِي وَ نُذُرِ).

We dealt thus with other nations who pursued the path of denial, pride, arrogance, sin, and rebellion; what do you think of yourselves as you continue in their path?

And again, at the end of this account, He adds: "And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?" Is there a listening ear for this divine call and these warnings and admonitions? (وَ لَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ).

It is noteworthy that the sentence "So how was My punishment and My warnings?" is repeated twice regarding the people of ‘Ad—once at the beginning of the narration of this story and once at its end. This difference is perhaps because the punishment of this group was more severe and terrifying than others, even though all divine punishments are severe.

Key Points Auspicious and Inauspicious Days

It is common among people to consider some days as "Sa‘d" (auspicious and blessed) and others as "Nahs" (ominous and inauspicious), although there is much disagreement in determining them. The question here is: to what extent is this general belief accepted in Islam, or derived from it?

Of course, from the perspective of reason, it is not impossible for parts of time to differ from one another—some possessing qualities of inauspiciousness and others possessing the opposite. Although we have no way to prove or deny such a matter through rational argument, we can say it is possible, but not proven by reason.

Therefore, if we have religious evidence through revelation—which illuminates broader horizons—regarding this meaning, accepting it is not only without obstacle but necessary.

In the verses of the Quran, "inauspiciousness of days" is mentioned in only two instances:

One is in the verses under discussion, and the other is in verse 16 of Surah Fussilat, which speaks about this same event of the people of ‘Ad. There we read: "So We sent upon them a screaming wind in days of ill-fortune" (فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحاً صَرْصَراً فِي أَيَّامٍ نَحِساتٍ). [1]

[1]: It should be noted that "Nahisat" (نَحِسات) in this verse is an adjective for "Ayyam" (days), meaning the said days are described as inauspicious. Whereas in the verses under discussion (فِي يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُسْتَمِرٍّ), "Yawm" (day) is in a possessive construction with "Nahs," and does not have an adjectival meaning. However, by the context of the aforementioned verse, we must say that here it is of the type of adding the "described" to the "attribute" (Note carefully).

Conversely, the term "Mubarak" (blessed) is also seen in some verses of the Quran, as He says regarding the Night of Power: "Indeed, We revealed it on a blessed night" (إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ) (Ad-Dukhan: 3).

As we said, "Nahs" originally means the extraordinary redness of the horizon which makes it appear as "Nuhas," meaning "a flame of fire without smoke"; subsequently, it was used in the sense of "ominous" for this reason.

Thus, the Quran has only a brief allusion to this issue. However, in Islamic traditions, we encounter many hadiths regarding the "inauspiciousness and auspiciousness of days." Although many of them are weak traditions or perhaps mixed with fabricated reports and superstitions, they are not all like that; rather, there are undoubtedly authentic and acceptable traditions among them, just as commentators have affirmed this meaning in the interpretation of the above verses.

The great traditionist, the late Allamah Majlisi, has also brought numerous traditions in Bihar al-Anwar in this regard. [1] What can be said here in a condensed and summarized form consists of several points:

A- In several traditions, the auspiciousness and inauspiciousness of days are interpreted in connection with the events that occurred therein. For example, in a tradition from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read that a person asked the Imam عليه السلام to explain regarding "Wednesday" and the bad omen people associate with it and its heaviness—which Wednesday is meant?

He replied: "It is the last Wednesday of the month, which occurs during the waning of the moon (Muhaq), and on this day Qabil killed his brother Habil... and on Wednesday Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, sent the wind upon the people of ‘Ad." [1]

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 59, Book of Heaven and the World, pp. 1-91 and a portion thereafter.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 183 (Hadith 25).

Therefore, many commentators, following many traditions, consider the last Wednesday of every month as an inauspicious day and refer to it as "Arba‘a La Tadūr" (the Wednesday that does not recur).

In some other traditions, we read that the first day of the month is an auspicious and blessed day because Adam was created on it. Likewise, the 26th day, because Allah parted the sea for Musa. [2] Or that the third day of the month is an inauspicious day because Adam and Hawwa were expelled from Paradise on that day, and the heavenly garments were stripped from them. [3] Or that the seventh day of the month is a blessed day because Nuh boarded the Ark (and was saved from the deluge). [4] Or regarding Nowruz, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "It is a blessed day when the Ark of Nuh settled upon [Mount] Judi, and Jibril descended upon the Prophet of Islam, and it is the day when Ali عليه السلام went upon the shoulders of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and broke the idols of the Kaaba, and the event of Ghadir Khum coincided with Nowruz..." [5]

Such expressions are frequent in traditions, linking the auspiciousness and inauspiciousness of days with certain desirable or undesirable events. Especially regarding the day of Ashura, which the Umayyads—thinking they had triumphed over the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام—considered an auspicious day. In traditions, seeking blessings from that day is strictly forbidden, and they have even commanded not to make that day a day for storing the year's provisions and the like; rather, one should cease business and work on that day and practically distance oneself from the program of the Umayyads.

The sum of these traditions has led some to interpret the issue of auspicious and inauspicious days such that the purpose of Islam is to draw the attention of Muslims to these events, so that in terms of action, they may align themselves with constructive historical events and distance themselves from destructive events and the methods of their founders.

This interpretation may be true for a portion of these traditions, but it is certainly not true for all of them, because it is understood from some of them that a mysterious influence occasionally exists in certain days of which we are unaware.

B: This point is also noteworthy: some have gone to such extremes in the matter of auspicious and inauspicious days that for every task they wish to undertake, they first seek out the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of the days, and practically remain held back from many activities, losing golden opportunities.

Or, instead of examining the factors of their own and others' failure and success and using these valuable experiences in life, they blame all failures on the inauspiciousness of the days, just as they seek the secret of victories in the goodness of the days! This is a kind of escape from reality, an excess in the matter, and a superstitious explanation of life's events that must be strictly avoided. In these matters, one should neither listen to rumors among the people, nor the words of astrologers, nor the sayings of fortune-tellers. If something is proven in an authentic hadith in this regard, it must be accepted; otherwise, one should continue the path of life indifferent to the sayings of this person or that, and move forward with firm steps through effort, striving, and hard work, seeking help through tawakkul (reliance) on Allah and assistance from His grace.

[2]: Ibid, p. 105. [3]: Ibid, p. 58. [4]: Ibid, p. 61. [5]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 59, p. 92.

C: The issue of paying attention to the auspiciousness and inauspiciousness of days, in addition to often guiding man toward a series of instructive historical events, is a factor for tawassul (recourse) and turning toward the Divine Presence, and seeking aid from the Pure Essence of the Lord. Therefore, we read in several traditions: on days labeled as inauspicious, you can proceed with your affairs and be victorious and successful by giving charity (Sadaqah), or reciting supplications, seeking aid from Allah's grace, reciting some verses of the Quran, and relying on His Pure Essence.

Among these, we read in a hadith that one of the friends of Imam Hasan al-Askari عليه السلام visited him on a Tuesday. The Imam said: "I did not see you yesterday." He replied: "It was Monday, and I disliked traveling on this day!" He said:

"Whoever wishes that Allah protect him from the evil of Monday should recite 'Hal Ata ‘ala al-Insan' (Surah al-Insan) in the first rak‘ah of the morning prayer." Then Abu al-Hasan (the Imam) عليه السلام recited this verse from Surah al-Insan (which is appropriate for removing evil): "So Allah protected them from the evil of that Day... Allah keeps the righteous from the evil of the Day of Resurrection and grants them radiance and happiness." [1]

In another hadith, we read that one of the companions of the sixth Imam عليه السلام asked him: "Is it appropriate to travel on any of the disliked days like Wednesday and others?" The Imam عليه السلام replied: "Begin your journey with charity and recite Ayat al-Kursi when you wish to move" (and go wherever you wish). [1]

In another hadith, it is narrated that one of the companions of the tenth Imam, Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi عليه السلام, says: "I visited the Imam عليه السلام while my finger had been injured on the way, a rider had passed by me and hurt my shoulder, and I got caught in a crowd and they tore my clothes. I said: 'May Allah protect me from your evil, O day! What an inauspicious day you are!' The Imam said: 'You associate with us and yet you say such a thing?! You blame the day which has no sin?' The man says: 'Upon hearing this, I came to my senses and realized my mistake. I said: O my master, I seek forgiveness and ask pardon from Allah.' The Imam عليه السلام added:

'What is the sin of the days that you consider them inauspicious when you are requited for your deeds therein?'

The narrator says: 'I said: I seek forgiveness from Allah forever, and this is my repentance, O son of the Messenger of Allah.'

The Imam عليه السلام said:

'This does not benefit you, but Allah punishes you for blaming that which deserves no blame. Do you not know that Allah is the One who rewards and punishes, and the One who requites for deeds, sooner or later?' I said: 'Yes, my master.' He said: 'Do not repeat this and do not attribute any influence to the days in the judgment of Allah!'" [1]

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 59, p. 39, Hadith 7. [1]: Ibid, p. 28. [1]: Tuhaf al-Uqul, as quoted in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 59, p. 2 (with slight summary).

This meaningful hadith points out that even if days have an influence, it is by the command of Allah; one must never attribute independent influence to them or consider oneself independent of Allah's grace. Furthermore, one should not link events—which often serve as an expiation for man's incorrect deeds—to the influence of days to exonerate oneself. Perhaps this explanation is the best way to reconcile the various reports on this subject [Note carefully].

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِٱلنُّذُرِ (٢٣)
[The people of] Thamud denied the warnings, (23)
فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَبَشَرًا مِّنَّا وَٰحِدًا نَّتَّبِعُهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ وَسُعُرٍ (٢٤)
and they said, ‘Are we to follow a lone human from ourselves?! Indeed then we would be in error and madness.’ (24)
أَءُلْقِىَ ٱلذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنۢ بَيْنِنَا بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ (٢٥)
‘Has the Reminder been cast upon him from among us? No, he is a self-conceited liar.’ (25)
سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَدًا مَّنِ ٱلْكَذَّابُ ٱلْأَشِرُ (٢٦)
‘Tomorrow they will know who is a self-conceited liar. (26)
إِنَّا مُرْسِلُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَّهُمْ فَٱرْتَقِبْهُمْ وَٱصْطَبِرْ (٢٧)
We are sending the She-camel as a trial for them; so watch them and be steadfast. (27)
وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ ٱلْمَآءَ قِسْمَةٌۢ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُّحْتَضَرٌ (٢٨)
Inform them that the water is to be shared between them; each of them showing up at his turn.’ (28)
فَنَادَوْا۟ صَاحِبَهُمْ فَتَعَاطَىٰ فَعَقَرَ (٢٩)
But they called their companion, and he took [a knife] and hamstrung [her]. (29)
فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (٣٠)
So how were My punishment and My warnings?! (30)
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَكَانُوا۟ كَهَشِيمِ ٱلْمُحْتَظِرِ (٣١)
We sent against them a single Cry, and they became like the dry sticks of a corral builder. (31)
وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (٣٢)
Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonishment. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (32)

Commentary

The Painful End of the People of Thamud

The third group whose life story is briefly presented as a lesson—following the previous discussions in this Surah—is the "People of Thamud." They lived in the land of "al-Hijr," located in the north of Hijaz. Their Prophet, "Salih" (peace be upon him), exerted his utmost effort to guide them, but to no avail.

First, He says: The tribe of Thamud also denied the divine warnings and treated the threats as naught: (كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِالنُّذُرِ).

Although some commentators have interpreted Nudhur here to mean "warning prophets" and considered Thamud’s denial of Salih as a denial of all prophets—since the call of all prophets was harmonious—it appears more likely that Nudhur here is the plural of Indhar, meaning "warnings" or statements coupled with frightening consequences, which naturally exist in the speech of every prophet.

Then, addressing the cause of their denial, He adds: "They said: Shall we follow a single human being from among ourselves? If we did so, we would surely be in error and madness" (فَقالُوا أَ بَشَراً مِنَّا واحِداً نَتَّبِعُهُ إِنَّا إِذاً لَفِي ضَلالٍ وَ سُعُرٍ). Indeed, pride, arrogance, and self-centeredness were the great veils before them against the call of the prophets. They would say: Salih is an individual just like us; there is no reason for us to follow him! What merit does he have over us to be a leader while we are his followers and subordinates? This is the same objection that misguided nations often raised against prophets: that they are individuals of our own kind and, for this reason, cannot be divine messengers.

A group of commentators has inferred from the expression Wahidan (single/one) that Salih’s enemies meant he was an ordinary person, lacking immense wealth or an extraordinary lineage. Others have interpreted it to mean he is a single person without a following or group, whereas a leader should have a multitude at his disposal and use that advantage to call others to follow him.

However, there is a third interpretation: they were not emphasizing numerical singularity, but rather a singularity of kind. Their point was that he is an individual of our own species, and the human species cannot bear a divine message; rather, a prophet should be of the genus of angels. Of course, a combination of all three interpretations is possible.

In any case, it was a hollow and baseless claim.

Su‘ur (rhyming with shatur) is the plural of Sa‘ir, originally meaning a blazing and raging fire. Sometimes it is used to mean "madness," because in a state of insanity, a person experiences a specific agitation; hence, a "mad camel" is called Naqah Mas‘urah.

It is also possible that the people of Thamud took this expression from their Prophet Salih (peace be upon him), who told them: "If you do not abandon idol-worship and follow my call, you will be in Dalal and Su‘ur (error and the blazing fires of Hell)." In response, they say: "If we follow a human like ourselves, we will be in Dalal and Su‘ur!" In any case, the mention of Su‘ur in the plural form is for emphasis and duration, whether it means madness or blazing fire.

Then they added that even assuming divine revelation could descend upon a human: "Has the revelation been sent down to him alone out of all of us?" while there are individuals more prominent, famous, and wealthier than him? (أَ أُلْقِيَ الذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ بَيْنِنا). In reality, the statements of the people of Thamud bore a great resemblance to the statements of the polytheists of Makkah, who would sometimes object: "Why does this messenger eat food and walk in the markets? Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner with him?" (Al-Furqan: 7).

And sometimes they said: "Why was this Quran not sent down to a great man from the two cities [Makkah and Ta’if]?" (Az-Zukhruf: 31).

If a divine messenger is to be a human, why a poor and solitary one? These blind-hearted people—who seemingly whispered the same rhetoric into each other's ears throughout history—imagined that if someone possessed wealth, a tribe, status, or a famous lineage, it was proof of their character, whereas the most oppressive people were often found among such individuals.

Some commentators have also accepted the possibility that they were saying: "Has revelation been sent only to him? Why is it not sent to all of us? What difference is there between us?" Just as it appears in verse 52 of Surah Al-Muddaththir: "Nay, every one of them desires that he should be given pages spread out!" Then, at the end of the verse, it says: "They took this very issue as proof of the falsehood of their Prophet Salih and said: He is an insolent, boastful liar!" (بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ).

Because he wants to rule over us, take everything into his grasp, and act according to his whims.

The word Ashir is an adjective derived from Ashar (rhyming with qamar), which means "intense joy coupled with vanity and self-indulgence."

But the Quran says in response to them: "Tomorrow they will know who is the insolent liar" (سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَداً مَنِ الْكَذَّابُ الْأَشِرُ). When the divine punishment arrives and crushes them, turning them into a handful of dust and ashes, and then the "punishment after death" also seizes them, they will understand to whom such attributes truly belong. It is clear that "tomorrow" (ghadan) refers to the near future, and this is a subtle and beautiful expression.

A question may arise here: at the time these verses were revealed, they had certainly already seen their punishment; so why say "tomorrow they will know who the insolent liar is"?

Two answers can be given: First, this statement was actually addressed to the Prophet of Allah, Salih (peace be upon him), and was spoken on that day and in those circumstances; it is certain that on that day, the punishment had not yet descended.

Second, "tomorrow" refers to the Day of Resurrection when everything will be clearly revealed (though the first interpretation is more fitting and consistent with the subsequent verses).

Another question arises: the polytheists of Thamud had realized the reality of Salih’s call even before the punishment descended and had witnessed his undeniable miracle; so why does it say "tomorrow they will know"?

The answer becomes clear by noting one point: knowledge always has levels. At some levels, the opponent may still deny, but it eventually reaches a stage where there is no room for denial, and everything is seen with one's own eyes. The "knowledge" intended here is this complete stage.

Then, referring to the story of the "She-Camel" which was sent as a miracle and a clear proof of the truth of Salih’s (peace be upon him) call, He adds: "We revealed to Salih that We are sending the she-camel as a trial for them; so watch them and be patient" (إِنَّا مُرْسِلُوا النَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَهُمْ فَارْتَقِبْهُمْ وَ اصْطَبِرْ).

The "She-Camel"—the female camel sent as Salih’s miracle—was certainly not an ordinary camel; rather, it possessed extraordinary characteristics. Among them, according to a famous narration, this camel emerged from the heart of a rock in the mountain to be a clear miracle before the stubborn deniers.

Fitnah, as we have said before, originally means putting gold into a crucible and fire to clarify its level of purity; subsequently, it was applied to any kind of trial or examination.

It is clear that the people of Thamud were placed before a great trial here. Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "We told Salih: Inform them that the water of the village must be divided among them (one day for the she-camel and one day for the people of the village), and each must attend at their own turn and not interfere with the other" (وَ نَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ الْماءَ قِسْمَةٌ بَيْنَهُمْ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُحْتَضَرٌ) [1]. Although the Quran does not provide more detail than this, many commentators have said that Salih’s she-camel would drink all the water on her turn. Others have said its appearance was such that when it came to the water, other animals would flee and not approach; thus, there was no choice but to allocate the water to the she-camel one day and to themselves the next.

[1]: Muhtadar is the passive participle from the root hudur (presence), and shirb means a share or turn of water. Therefore, the sentence kullu shirbin muhtadar means that each turn is attended by its owner, and the other has no right to be present or interfere.

In any case, this people faced a water shortage and could not tolerate the water being entirely at the disposal of the "She-Camel" one day and themselves the next, especially according to the possibility suggested by some commentators that water was very scarce in their settlement (though this meaning does not align well with verses 146 to 148, which imply this people lived in a land full of gardens and springs).

In any case, this rebellious, selfish, and stubborn people decided to kill the "She-Camel," even though Salih had warned them that if they harmed the camel, punishment would seize them in a short time. However, without heeding this, "they called their companion, and he took [the task] and hamstrung [her]" (فَنادَوْا صاحِبَهُمْ فَتَعاطى‌ فَعَقَرَ). "Companion" (sahib) here may refer to one of the leaders of the people of Thamud, one of their famous villains recorded in history as "Qudar bin Salif" [1].

Ta‘ati originally means taking something or going after a matter; it is also used for performing important and dangerous tasks, or laborious tasks, or a task for which a reward or wage has been set.

All these interpretations are combined in the verse under discussion, as the act of killing the she-camel required great audacity and boldness, was a laborious task, and presumably, a reward had been set for it.

‘Aqr is from the root ‘aqr (rhyming with zulm), which originally means the base or root.

[1]: Qudar (rhyming with manarah) was a man of ugly appearance and character, and one of the most ill-omened individuals in history.

When this word is used regarding a camel, it means killing, slaughtering, or hamstringing.

It is noteworthy that the Quran here attributes the killing of the she-camel to one individual, whereas in Surah Ash-Shams, it attributes it to all of them, saying: fa ‘aqaruha (the people of Thamud killed the she-camel). This is because that one person acted as a representative and with the consent of the entire people. We know that whoever is pleased with the action of another is a partner in that deed [1]. In some narrations, it is mentioned that "Qudar" first drank wine and, in a state of intoxication, committed this foul act and great crime.

Regarding the method of killing the she-camel, there are various accounts: some say he hamstrung it with a sword, and others say he lay in wait behind a rock, first shot it with an arrow, and then attacked it with a sword.

The next verse, as a prelude to mentioning the terrifying punishment of this rebellious people, says: "Now see how was My punishment and My warnings!" (فَكَيْفَ كانَ عَذابِي وَ نُذُرِ).

And then He adds: "We sent upon them a single blast, and they became like the dry, crushed stalks that a fence-builder gathers for his animals in a pen!" (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً فَكانُوا كَهَشِيمِ الْمُحْتَظِرِ).

[1]: We explained this concept under the title "Ideological Bond" in Volume 9, page 158 (under verse 65 of Surah Hud).

Sayhah here means a mighty sound rising from the sky, and it may refer to a terrifying lightning bolt that broke out over their city, as mentioned in verse 13 of Surah Fussilat: "But if they turn away, then say: I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud!" Hashim is from the root hashm (rhyming with khashm), which originally means breaking fragile objects like plants. Thus, hashim refers to crushed plants that sheep owners crush and prepare for them; sometimes it is interpreted as dry plants crushed under the feet of animals in a pen.

Muhtazir is originally from the root hazr (rhyming with maghz), meaning "prevention." Therefore, a pen made for sheep and animals to prevent them from leaving or to prevent attacks by wild animals is called a hazirah, and a muhtazir (rhyming with muhtasib) is the person who owns such a place.

The description given in this verse regarding the punishment of the people of Thamud is very strange and meaningful. For Allah never sent armies from the heavens or the earth to destroy this rebellious people; with only a single heavenly blast, a deafening thunderbolt, a massive explosion wave that crushed and pulverized everything in its path over a wide radius, He finished them! Their palaces and inhabited houses became like a sheep "pen," and their lifeless, broken bodies became like crushed dry plants strewn under the feet of sheep.

Understanding this was difficult for people of the past, but for us today, who are aware of the impact of blast waves and how they disintegrate and crush everything in their path, it is easy to understand. Of course, the thunderbolt of divine punishment is not comparable to these human explosions. From this, it becomes clear what calamity this great thunderbolt brought upon this headstrong people.

In the final verse under discussion and at the end of this painful and lesson-filled story, He says once more: "And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance and awakening; so is there any who will take heed?" (وَ لَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ). Its expressions are vivid and clear, its stories are eloquent, and its warnings and threats are soul-shaking and awakening.

Ayahs
