Commentary

The People of Lot Suffered a More Sinister Fate

In these verses, there are brief and jarring references to the story of the people of Lot and the horrific punishment of this disgraceful and misguided population. This is the fourth segment regarding the history of past nations mentioned in this Surah.

First, it states: "The people of Lot denied the successive warnings of their Prophet" (كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ بِالنُّذُرِ).

"Nuthur" (نُذُر), as we previously mentioned, is the plural of "Inthar" (إِنذار), meaning threat and warning. Its mention in the plural form may refer to the successive warnings of this great Prophet, all of which this stubborn people denied, or it may refer to the warnings of Prophet Lot (عليه السلام) and the Prophets before him, as the calls of the Prophets all pursue a single truth.

Then, in a short sentence, it refers to a portion of their punishment and the salvation of Prophet Lot's family, saying: "Indeed, We sent upon them a storm of stones" (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا).

And We buried them all under this rain of pebbles.

"Except the family of Lot; We saved them at dawn" (إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ نَّجَّيْنَاهُم بِسَحَرٍ).

"Hasib" (حَاصِب) refers to a violent wind that moves "Hasba" (حَصْباء), meaning pebbles and stones.

In other verses of the Quran, when enumerating the punishment of the people of Lot, in addition to the earthquake that overturned their cities, it speaks of a rain of stones, as we read in Surah Hud, verse 82: فَلَمَّا جَاءَ أَمْرُنَا جَعَلْنَا عَالِيَهَا سَافِلَهَا وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ مَّنضُودٍ: "So when Our command came, We made the highest part [of the city] its lowest and rained upon them stones of layered baked clay."

Were these two types of punishment—one a violent wind that picked up desert stones and pebbles and struck them, and the other a rain of celestial stones—or do both refer to the same thing? For sometimes massive whirlwinds in deserts lift piles of stones and sand from the ground into the sky, and when the pressure of the whirlwind subsides, it suddenly drops them onto another point. It is not unlikely that here, too, by Allah's command, a whirlwind was commissioned to carry massive piles of desert stones and sand into the sky and, after the devastating earthquake, pour them down upon the cities of the people of Lot, burying their defiled bodies beneath them and even erasing the ruins of their cities from the face of the earth to serve as a lesson for others forever. [1]

[1]: We have had other discussions on this subject in Volume 9, page 190 onwards (under verse 82 of Surah Hud).

Furthermore, the above verse says: the salvation of Prophet Lot's family was at dawn. This was because the promised punishment for this oppressive people was also in the morning. Therefore, at the end of the night, by Allah's command, this faithful family (except for his wife, who had separated her path from Lot's) left the city, and it was not long before the earthquake and then the rain of stones began, as stated in the verse of Surah Hud: فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ اللَّيْلِ وَلَا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنكُمْ أَحَدٌ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَكَ إِنَّهُ مُصِيبُهَا مَا أَصَابَهُمْ إِنَّ مَوْعِدَهُمُ الصُّبْحُ أَلَيْسَ الصُّبْحُ بِقَرِيبٍ: "So travel with your family in a portion of the night and let not any of you look back—except your wife; indeed, she will be struck by that which strikes them. Indeed, their appointment is [for] the morning. Is not the morning near?" From this, it becomes clear that the interpretation by some commentators, following certain lexicographers, of "Sahar" (سحر) here as the time "between dawn and sunrise" has no relevance to the above verse. [1]

In the next verse, for emphasis, He says: "The salvation of Lot's family was a favor from Us; thus do We reward whoever is grateful" (نِّعْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا كَذَلِكَ نَجْزِي مَن شَكَرَ). [2]

And in the following verse, it recounts the fact that Lot had previously completed the proof (atmam al-hujjah) against them.

[1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Sahar is the mixing of the darkness of the end of the night with the light of the day."

[2]: "Ni'matan" (نِعْمَةً) is an object (maf'ul bihi) for a hidden verb of its own kind, or a "maf'ul lahu" (object of purpose) for the verb "najjaynahum" (We saved them) mentioned in the previous verse.

"And he had already warned them of Our assault, but they disputed the warnings" (وَلَقَدْ أَنذَرَهُم بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا بِالنُّذُرِ).

"Batsh" (بَطْش) originally means seizing something with power, and since a criminal is seized with power before punishment, this word has also come to mean punishment.

"Tamaru" (تَمَارَوْا) is from the root "tamari," meaning mutual dialogue intended to create doubt, cast suspicion, and argue against the truth. In reality, they joined hands and entered through various means to create doubt and suspicion in public opinion to neutralize the effects of this great Prophet's warnings.

They did not stop at casting ideological doubts among the people; rather, they reached such a point of depravity, insolence, and shamelessness that when the angels commissioned for punishment entered Lot's house in the form of handsome young men as guests, these shameless people came after them and, as the next verse says, "they demanded his guests from him" (وَلَقَدْ رَاوَدُوهُ عَن ضَيْفِهِ).

Lot was so distressed by this matter that it knew no bounds, and he insistently begged them to stop this scandal and disgrace. According to verse 71 of Surah Al-Hijr, he even promised to give his daughters to them in marriage (should they repent from these acts). This shows the ultimate victimhood of this great Prophet among this shameless, faithless group that lacked everything.

But it was not long before this attacking group received their first punishment, as Allah says in the continuation of the same verse: "So We blinded their eyes and said, 'Taste My punishment and My warnings'" (فَطَمَسْنَا أَعْيُنَهُمْ فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ).

Yes, it was here that the hand of Allah's power emerged from the sleeve of justice. According to some, He commanded Gabriel to strike their eyes with a corner of his wing; immediately, they all became blind. It is even said that the place of their eyes became completely smooth, just like their faces! Although the verse does not mention which individuals came after the angels, it was certainly not all the people of Lot; rather, the thugs who surpassed others in insolence took the lead in this act, and their fate became a lesson for others, as they returned to the crowd in that miserable state. However, there was no heart ready among them to take a lesson from this preliminary punishment.

It is said that the reason Allah delayed their final punishment until sunrise was that this incident took place the day before, and He gave them the opportunity to think about their fate for one more night and see an example of divine punishment in these miserable blind men, perhaps they would wake up and repent. But alas, according to a narration, even these blind men did not take a lesson; when they held onto the walls and returned to their homes, they swore that by tomorrow morning, they would not leave a single member of Lot's family alive. [1]

Finally, the ultimate punishment arrived. With a devastating earthquake that shook that land with the first rays of dawn, their cities were overturned, bodies were torn apart and disintegrated under the rubble, and a heavy rain of stones fell upon them, erasing even the traces of their ruins! As the next verse refers to this meaning in a concise and condensed manner:

"And there certainly came upon them in the early morning an abiding punishment" (وَلَقَدْ صَبَّحَهُم بُكْرَةً عَذَابٌ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ).

Yes, in a few short moments, everything ended, and no trace of them remained.

The expression "Bukratan" (بُكْرَةً - early morning) is used because "Sabbahahum" (صَبَّحَهُم) has a broad meaning covering the entire morning, whereas the intention is the beginning of the morning.

Was this event at the start of dawn (fajr) or the start of sunrise? It is not exactly clear, but perhaps the term "Bukratan" is more suitable for the start of sunrise.

The word "Mustaqirr" (مُّسْتَقِرٌّ) means stable and established. Here, it may refer to the fact that this punishment was so crushing, strong, and powerful that no force could oppose it.

It has also been said that because this worldly punishment was connected to their purgatorial (Barzakh) punishment, it was described as "Mustaqirr."

Then He adds: Once again they were told, "So taste My punishment and My warnings" (فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ). So that you may no longer doubt the warnings of the Prophets.

Although this sentence is mentioned twice in this account, it is evident that the first sentence refers to that preliminary punishment—the blinding of the group that stormed Lot's house—and the second time refers to the final punishment—the devastating earthquake and the rain of stones.

And finally, in the last verse under discussion, for the fourth time in this Surah, this meaningful and awakening sentence is repeated: "And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?" (وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ).

The people of Lot did not take heed—neither from the warnings nor from the preliminary and cautionary punishment. Will others who are tainted by the same sins come to their senses upon hearing these verses of the Quran, feel regret, and repent?!

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 185.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ ٱلنُّذُرُ (٤١)
Certainly the warnings did come to Pharaoh’s clan (41)
كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا كُلِّهَا فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ (٤٢)
who denied all of Our signs. So We seized them with the seizing of One [who is] all-mighty, Omnipotent. (42)
أَكُفَّارُكُمْ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ أَمْ لَكُم بَرَآءَةٌ فِى ٱلزُّبُرِ (٤٣)
Are your faithless better than those? Have you [been granted] [some sort of] immunity in the scriptures? (43)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ نَحْنُ جَمِيعٌ مُّنتَصِرٌ (٤٤)
Do they say, ‘We are a confederate league’? (44)
سَيُهْزَمُ ٱلْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ ٱلدُّبُرَ (٤٥)
The league will be routed and turn its back [to flee]. (45)
بَلِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ مَوْعِدُهُمْ وَٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْهَىٰ وَأَمَرُّ (٤٦)
Indeed, the Hour is their tryst; and the Hour will be most calamitous and bitter. (46)

Commentary

Are You Superior to the Nations of the Past?!

The fifth and final people mentioned in this series is the people of Pharaoh. However, since the history of this people has been detailed in various surahs of the Quran, only a brief and concise reference to their cautionary tale is made here.

It says: "Our warnings came (one after another) to the people of Pharaoh" (وَلَقَدْ جَاءَ آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ النُّذُرُ).

The term "Al-Pharaoh" (آل فرعون) does not refer only to his family and relatives, but includes his followers in general. Although "Al" is often used for "household and family," it is occasionally used in the broader sense mentioned, and the context indicates that this broader meaning is intended here.

"Nudhur" (نُذُر) is the plural of "Nadhir" (نذیر), meaning a warner, whether it be a human or an event that warns people and cautions them against the consequences of their actions. In the first case, the verse may refer to Musa and Harun (peace be upon them), and in the second case, it may refer to the nine miracles of Musa. However, the following verse indicates that the second meaning is more appropriate here. [^1]

The next verse reveals the reaction of the "People of Pharaoh" toward these two great Prophets of Allah and their warnings, stating: "They denied all Our signs" (كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا كُلِّهَا).

Yes, these self-centered arrogant ones, and these autocratic and oppressive tyrants, denied all of Allah's signs without exception; they considered them all to be lies, sorcery, or accidental occurrences! "Signs" (Ayat) has a broad meaning that includes intellectual proofs, miracles, and transmitted evidences. However, based on the evidence of verse 101 of Surah Al-Isra: "And We certainly gave Musa nine clear signs" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ تِسْعَ آيَاتٍ بَيِّنَاتٍ), it becomes clear that the reference here is to those same nine miracles. [^1] If a person is a seeker of truth, seeing one of these miracles—preceded by a warning and followed by the removal of the affliction through the prayer of Allah's Prophet—would be sufficient for him. But when a person becomes stubborn, even if the entire heavens and earth become a sign of God, it will not be effective; only the divine punishment must arrive to crush the minds filled with the wind of arrogance.

As we read at the end of the verse under discussion: "So We seized them with the seizure of One who is All-Mighty and All-Powerful" (فَأَخَذْنَاهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ).

"Akhdh" (أخذ) originally means taking or seizing, but since a criminal is first apprehended to be punished, this word is used as a metaphor for punishment.

The expression used at the end of this story is unique and has no parallel in other accounts. This is because the Pharaohs boasted of their power and might more than anyone else, and everywhere there was talk of the strength of their government. However, Allah says: We seized them, the seizure of One who is All-Mighty and All-Powerful, so it would be known that this hollow power and might are nothingness in the face of the Might and Power of Allah. It is remarkable that the same great Nile River, which was the source of all their wealth, power, prosperity, and civilization, was commissioned to destroy them. Even more remarkable is that small creatures like locusts, frogs, and lice (a type of plant pest) overcame them, exhausted them, and made them wretched.

After recounting the stories of previous nations and the punishment of rebellious and criminal peoples, the next verse addresses the polytheists of Makkah, saying: "Are your disbelievers better than those [previous ones], or have you an immunity [written] in the scriptures?" (أَكُفَّارُكُمْ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أُولَٰئِكُمْ أَمْ لَكُم بَرَاءَةٌ فِي الزُّبُرِ) [^1] What is the difference between you and the people of Pharaoh, the people of Nuh, Lut, and Thamud? If they were afflicted by storms, earthquakes, and lightning because of their disbelief, rebellion, injustice, and sin, what reason is there that you should not suffer such a fate?! Are you better than them? Or is your rebellion, enmity, and disbelief less than theirs?

[^1]: It should be noted that "Nudhur" is both the plural of "Nadhir" and has a gerundial or verbal noun meaning; since the gerund is also used in a descriptive sense, both can return to a single concept.

[^1]: Based on various verses of the Quran, the "Nine Miracles of Musa" are: 1- The staff turning into a great serpent (Taha-20). 2- The "White Hand" and the shining of Musa's hand like a source of light (Taha-22). 3- Crushing storms (A'raf-133). 4, 5, 6, and 7- "Locusts" that dominated the crops, "Qummal" (a type of plant pest), "Dafadi'" (frogs that rose from the Nile and covered their living space in a short time), and "Dam" (the Nile River turning the color of blood) (A'raf-133). 8 and 9- "Drought" and shortage of various fruits (A'raf-130), the details of each being given under the relevant verses.

[^1]: The pronoun in "Kuffarukum" (your disbelievers) apparently refers to the "Arab polytheists," as evidenced by the phrase "or have you an immunity in the scriptures."

So how do you consider yourselves immune from divine punishment? Unless an immunity has been sent for you in the heavenly books, and certainly such a claim is a lie and you have no evidence for it.

"Or do they say: 'We are a united and strong assembly, and we shall take revenge on our opponents and be victorious over them'?" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ نَحْنُ جَمِيعٌ مُّنتَصِرٌ) [^1] "Jami'" means a "collective," and the intention here is a group that has a goal and the power to act, and the expression "Muntasir" is an emphasis on this meaning, as it comes from the root "Intisar," meaning taking revenge and being victorious. It is noteworthy that the previous verse was in the form of an "address" (second person), but the verse under discussion and the future verses speak of the disbelievers in the "absent" (third person) form. This is a type of belittlement, meaning they are not worthy of being addressed further by the Divine speech.

In any case, if they claim such power, that too is a baseless claim, for the "People of Pharaoh" and the "People of 'Ad and Thamud" and their like, who were stronger than these, showed the least resistance against divine punishment—like a blade of straw before a massive storm—let alone this small group lacking everything!

Then, as a definitive prophecy to refute their words, it adds: let them know "The assembly will soon be defeated, and they will turn their backs [in flight]" (سَيُهْزَمُ الْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ الدُّبُرَ). [^1] Interestingly, "Sayuhzamu" comes from the root "Hazm," which originally means pressing a dry object to the point that it disintegrates, and for this reason, it is used for the disintegration and breaking of an army.

This expression may be an allusion to the point that although they appear united and cohesive, because they are dry beings lacking flexibility, they will break under a strong pressure. In contrast, the believers possess a firmness combined with flexibility; if the gale of events bends their backs, they soon stand tall again and face the incident once more.

"Dubur" means the back, as opposed to "Qubul," which means the front, and the mention of this word here is to describe turning one's back to the battlefield completely.

This prophecy was fulfilled on the field of Badr and other Islamic battles, and finally, the powerful assembly of disbelievers disintegrated and fled.

In the final verse under discussion, it adds: failure and disappointment in this world are not their only portion, rather "But the Hour is their appointment, and the Hour is more calamitous and more bitter" (بَلِ السَّاعَةُ مَوْعِدُهُمْ وَالسَّاعَةُ أَدْهَىٰ وَأَمَرُّ). Thus, they must expect both a bitter defeat in this world and an even more bitter and terrifying defeat in the Hereafter.

"Adha" comes from the root "Dahw" and "Daha'," meaning a great calamity and event for which there is no escape, remedy, or cure. Sometimes it also means extreme cleverness, but in the verse under discussion, it carries the first meaning.

Yes, in the Resurrection, they will be caught in such a fate that there is no way to flee from it.

Key Points A Clear and Miraculous Prophecy

Undoubtedly, at the time these verses were revealed in Makkah, the Muslims were in a severe minority and the enemy was at the peak of power, and the prediction of a swift victory for the Muslims over them was never believable. However, a short time passed, the Muslims migrated, and they gained so much power that in their first armed encounter with the enemy on the field of Badr, they dealt them a very heavy and massive blow, such as they never expected. Not many years passed after the event of Badr before not only the disbelievers of Makkah but the entire Arabian Peninsula surrendered to them! Is this news of the unseen regarding the future, especially with such decisiveness and clarity, not a miracle?

We know that one aspect of the Quran's miracle is its inclusion of news of the unseen, of which the verse under discussion is an example.

[^1]: Although "Nahnu" is a plural pronoun, its predicate "Jami'" appears in the singular, as does "Muntasir," which is a predicate after a predicate or an adjective for "Jami'"; this is because "Jami'," though singular in form, is plural in meaning.

[^1]: Although it would be appropriate to say "Yuwalluna al-Adbar," it says "Yuwalluna al-Dubur" instead, because this word has a generic meaning which functions as a plural.

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَنَهَرٍ (٥٤)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and streams, (54)
فِى مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِندَ مَلِيكٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍۭ (٥٥)
in the abode of truthfulness with an omnipotent King. (55)

Commentary

In the Seat of Sincerity Before the All-Powerful Lord!

These verses are, in fact, a continuation of the discussion in the preceding verses regarding the state of the polytheists and criminals on the Day of Resurrection. The final verse of the previous section established the truth that the Day of Resurrection is their appointed time—a day of calamity and bitterness, more bitter than the misfortunes and defeats of this world.

In the first verse under discussion, the reason for this is explained, stating:

"Indeed, the criminals are in error and madness [or blazing fires]" (إِنَّ الْمُجْرِمِينَ فِي ضَلالٍ وَ سُعُرٍ) [1]

"The Day they are dragged into the Fire on their faces [it will be said], 'Taste the touch of Saqar!'"—the Hell that you constantly denied and considered a lie and a myth (يَوْمَ يُسْحَبُونَ فِي النَّارِ عَلى‌ وُجُوهِهِمْ ذُوقُوا مَسَّ سَقَرَ).

"Saqar" (rhyming with safar) originally means the changing of the body's color and being pained by the sun's rays and the like. Since Hell possesses this transformation and torment in its most intense form, one of its names is "Saqar." The word "mass" refers to contact and touching. Therefore, the denizens of Hell are told: "Taste the scorching heat resulting from the touch of the fire of Hell; taste it and do not say these are lies, superstitions, or myths."

Some believe that "Saqar" is not the name for the entirety of Hell, but rather a specific section of it that possesses an extraordinarily strange burning intensity. As narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "There is a valley called 'Saqar' which is the abode of the arrogant; whenever it breathes, it scorches Hell itself!" [1]

Since it might be imagined that these punishments are not proportionate to those sins, the next verse adds: "Indeed, all things We created with predestination [measure]" (إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلَقْناهُ بِقَدَرٍ). Yes, both their painful punishments in this world are based on a reckoning, and their severe punishments in the Hereafter. Not only the punishments, but everything Allah has created is based on a calculated system and order. The earth and the heavens, living and non-living beings, the organs of the human body, and the means and instruments of life—each is based on a necessary calculation and measure. Nothing in this universe is without reckoning and order, for it is the creation of a Wise Creator.

Then He says: Not only are Our acts based on wisdom, but they are also accompanied by ultimate power and decisiveness, for "Our command is but one, like the twinkling of an eye!" (وَ ما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ). Yes, whatever We will, We say to it: "Kun" (Be!), and it immediately comes into existence. (Even the word "Kun" is used due to the limitations of human expression; otherwise, Allah's Will and Intention are identical to the realization of His purpose).

Therefore, on the day We issue the command for the Resurrection to begin, with the blink of an eye, everything will be set on the path of the Resurrection, and new life will be breathed into the bodies.

Similarly, on the day We will to punish the criminals through lightning and heavenly cries, or earthquakes, storms, and hurricanes, or when We intend their punishment, only one command is necessary; immediately, all of this occurs.

All of these are warnings for the sinners so they may know that Allah, while being Wise, is Decisive, and while being Decisive, is Wise; thus, they should beware of opposing His command.

The next verse once again addresses the criminals and disbelievers, turning their attention to the fate of previous nations, saying: "And We have already destroyed your likes [in the past]; so is there any who will remember?" (وَ لَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنا أَشْياعَكُمْ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ).

"Ashya‘" is the plural of "Shi‘ah," meaning those who follow someone in every direction and spread and publicize matters related to him and strengthen him. If "Shi‘ah" is used to mean "follower," it is for this reason. Certainly, the previous nations were not followers of the polytheists of Mecca and their ilk; rather, the case was the opposite. However, since the supporters of a person are "similar" to him, this word is also used in the sense of "similar and like."

It should also not be overlooked that this group of Meccan disbelievers drew strength from the programs of their predecessors and utilized the line of their school of thought; for this reason, the previous nations are referred to as ashya‘ (likes/followers). In any case, this verse once again emphasizes the truth that when your actions, behavior, and beliefs are the same as theirs, there is no reason for you to have a fate other than theirs. Wake up and take heed!

Then it points to this fundamental principle: that with the death of previous nations, their deeds were not destroyed; rather, "And everything they did is in the records" (وَ كُلُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَعَلُوهُ فِي الزُّبُرِ). In the same way, your deeds are also recorded and registered, preserved for the Day of Reckoning.

"Zubur" is the plural of "Zabur," meaning "book," and here it refers to the records of human deeds. Some have also suggested it refers to the "Preserved Tablet" (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), but this meaning does not align with the plural form.

Then, for further emphasis, it adds: "And every small and great [thing] is inscribed" (وَ كُلُّ صَغِيرٍ وَ كَبِيرٍ مُسْتَطَرٌ). Therefore, the reckoning of deeds on that day is a comprehensive and complete reckoning. As when the record of deeds is given to the criminals, they will cry out: "Woe to us! What is this book that leaves nothing small or great except that it has enumerated it?" (وَ يَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها) (Surah Al-Kahf, Verse 49).

"Mustatar" is derived from the root "satr," which originally means "row" or "line," whether it be humans standing in a row, trees arranged in a line, or words lined up on a page. Since it is most commonly used in the latter sense, this meaning usually comes to mind. In any case, this is another warning to these sinful, negligent, and unaware individuals.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 23, p. 79

*Since the tradition of the Holy Quran is to introduce the righteous and the wicked, the good and the evil, in comparison to one another—because differences are more striking and evident in comparison—here too, after mentioning the fate of the criminal disbelievers, it makes a brief reference to the joyful and soul-nourishing fate of the pious, saying:

"Indeed, the righteous will be among gardens and rivers [of Paradise], in a vast space and the extensive grace of Allah" (إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَ نَهَرٍ).

"Nahar" (rhyming with qamar) and also "nahr" (rhyming with qahr) both mean a channel of abundant water. For this reason, sometimes a vast space or great grace and widespread light is called "nahar."

This word in the verse under discussion (regardless of the upcoming Hadith) could mean its original sense, i.e., a river of water, and its singular form poses no problem as it carries a generic and collective meaning; thus, it harmonizes with "jannat" (gardens), the plural of "jannah."

It could also refer to the vastness of Divine grace and the widespread light and brightness of Paradise and its expansive space, or both meanings. However, in a Hadith narrated in Al-Durr al-Manthur from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Al-nahar is space and vastness, not a flowing river." [1]

The final verse under discussion, which is the last verse of Surah al-Qamar, provides more explanation for the station of the pious, saying: "In a seat of sincerity [and truth] in the presence of a Sovereign, Perfect in Ability" (فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ). What a beautiful description of the station of the pious is given in this verse! It possesses two characteristics that encompass all privileges: First, it is a "seat of sincerity" where no falsehood or vanity can enter; it is entirely truth, and all of Allah's promises regarding Paradise become reality there, and their truthfulness becomes manifest.

Second: It is in the proximity and nearness of Allah, which is understood from the word "‘ind" (near/with), referring to the ultimate spiritual proximity and nearness, not physical. Furthermore, it is in relation to a Lord who is both the Sovereign (Malik) and the All-Powerful (Muqtadir). Every blessing and favor is within the grasp of His power and under the command of His governance and sovereignty. For this reason, He will not fall short in hosting these honorable guests, and only He knows what favors He has prepared for them.

It is noteworthy that in these two verses discussing the favors and rewards of the people of Paradise, He first speaks of their material favors—vast gardens and flowing rivers—and then of their great spiritual reward, which is presence in the proximity of the Sovereign, Perfect in Ability. This is to prepare the human step by step, allowing the soul to soar and be immersed in joy and vigor, especially since expressions like "Malik" (Sovereign), "Muqtadir" (All-Powerful), and "Maq‘ad Sidq" (Seat of Sincerity) all denote the permanence and eternity of this presence and spiritual proximity.

Key Points

1- Everything in the Universe is Based on Calculation The phrase "Indeed, all things We created with predestination [measure]" (إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلَقْناهُ بِقَدَرٍ), despite its brevity, unveils an important truth in the world of creation—a truth that governs the entire universe: precise measurement in all created things.

The more human science and knowledge progress, the more they become acquainted with this precise measurement. This precise measurement governs not only microscopic organisms but also the massive celestial bodies. For instance, we hear that astronauts succeed through precise scientific calculations performed by hundreds of specialized experts using electronic brains, landing exactly in the region of the Moon they intended. This is despite the fact that during the few days the spacecraft travels between Earth and Moon, everything changes: the Moon rotates on its axis and orbits the Earth, and its position changes completely. Even at the moment of launch, the Earth is moving both on its axis and around the Sun at extraordinary speeds. Yet, because all these various movements have a precise calculation and measure from which they never deviate, astronauts succeed in landing exactly in the target area! Astronomers can accurately predict partial and total lunar and solar eclipses occurring at various points on Earth decades in advance. These are all signs of precision in the measurements of the great universe.

In small creatures, in tiny ants with those complex and diverse organs, veins, and nerves, the delicacy of this measurement is staggering. When we reach even smaller beings—microscopic organisms, microbes, viruses, amoebas—the delicacy of this measurement reaches its peak, where even a thousandth of a millimeter and smaller is under calculation. Beyond that, when we enter the interior of the atom, all these scales of measurement must be discarded, and the measures become so small they cannot be grasped by any human mind.

This measurement is not only in the matter of quantities; the qualities of compositions also precisely benefit from this measurement. The system governing the human soul, psychology, desires, and instincts also has a precise measurement in the path of individual and social human needs; if the slightest change occurred in it, the system of his individual and social life would collapse.

In the natural world, there are creatures that are pests to one another, each acting as a brake for the other. Birds of prey feed on the meat of small birds, preventing them from exceeding their limits and damaging all crops; hence, they have long lives. Yet these same birds of prey lay very few eggs and produce few chicks, living only under specific conditions. If they were to produce many chicks with such long lives, it would be the end of all small birds. This issue has a very vast scope in the world of animals and plants, the study of which makes one more familiar with the depth of "Indeed, all things We created with predestination."

2- Divine Decree and Human Free Will A misconception or misuse might arise from the verse under discussion and its likes: that if Allah created everything according to a measure and calculation, then our deeds and actions are also His creation, and therefore we have no choice of our own.

However, as we have stated before, although our deeds are by Divine decree and will and are never outside the scope of His power and intention, He has decreed that we be autonomous in our actions. Therefore, He has established duty and responsibility for us; if we had no choice, duty and responsibility would be meaningless and nonsensical. Thus, if we were devoid of will and compelled in our actions, this would be contrary to Divine decree.

Conversely, in opposition to the excess of the "Determinists" (Jabriyyun), a group has fallen into the opposite extreme, called the "Qadariyyun" and the "Mu fawwidah." They explicitly say: "Our deeds are in our hands, and Allah has absolutely nothing to do with our actions." In this way, they limit the realm of Divine governance, imagine themselves independent, and follow the path of polytheism (shirk).

The truth is that reconciling these two principles (Monotheism and Justice) requires specific precision and delicacy. If we interpret Monotheism to mean that the Creator of everything, even our deeds, is Allah in such a way that we have no choice, we have denied Allah's "Justice," for He would be compelling sinners to commit sins and then punishing them. And if we interpret Justice to mean that Allah has no involvement in our actions, we have removed Him from His governance and fallen into the abyss of "polytheism."

The "matter between the two matters" (Amr bayn al-Amrayn), which is pure faith, the Straight Path, and the middle line, is to believe that we are autonomous, but our being autonomous is also by the will of Allah, and at any moment He wishes, He can strip us of that choice. This is the school of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام.

It is noteworthy that under the verses discussed, several narrations condemning these two groups have been recorded in both Sunni and Shiite commentary books. Among them is a Hadith where the Holy Prophet said: "Two groups from my Ummah have no share in Islam: the Murji'ah and the Qadariyyah; a verse was revealed about them in the Book of Allah: 'Indeed, the criminals are in error and madness...'" [1] "Murji'ah" is from the root irja’, meaning to postpone. This is a term used for the Determinists because they ignore Divine commands and turn toward sin under the assumption that they are compelled, or they believe the fate of those who commit major sins is unclear and postpone it to the Resurrection. [1] In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "This was revealed regarding the Qadariyyah: 'Taste the touch of Saqar! Indeed, all things We created with predestination.'" [2] (Indicating that the meaning of measure and calculation is that We have determined a precise punishment for every sin—this is another interpretation of the verse—or that you who denied Divine decree and thought yourselves capable of everything and thought Allah was outside the realm of your actions, now see the power of Allah and taste the punishment of your deviation).

3- Allah's Command is Only One Word! We know that between the "Complete Cause" and the "Effect," there is no temporal gap. Therefore, in philosophical terminology, the precedence of the cause over the effect is considered a "rank precedence." Regarding Allah's will concerning the act of creation and origination—which is the clearest instance of a complete cause—this meaning is even clearer.

Thus, if the verse "And Our command is but one" (وَ ما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ) has been interpreted as the word "Kun" (Be!), it is due to the limitations of language. Otherwise, the word "Kun" is composed of the letters Kaf and Nun and requires time. Even the "fa" in "fayakun" (and it is), which usually indicates a type of temporal sequence, is due to the limitations of expression. Even the comparison "like the twinkling of an eye" (كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ) [1] in verse 77 of Surah al-Nahl, when speaking of the Divine command regarding the Resurrection, adds: "or it is even closer!" (أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ). In any case, the mention of time here is for the sake of our everyday expressions and because the Quran speaks to us in our language; otherwise, Allah and His commands are above time. Additionally, the expression "one" (wahidah) may indicate that one command is sufficient and needs no repetition, or it may indicate that His command regarding the small and the great, and even the creation of the entirety of the vast heavens, does not differ from a single grain of dust. Essentially, small and great, difficult and easy, are within the scale of our limited thought and meager power; where infinite power is concerned, these concepts vanish entirely, and all become of one color and form (reflect on this).

Here, a "question" arises: if the meaning of the above sentence is that everything comes into existence instantaneously, this does not seem to align with the observation of the gradual nature of worldly events. The "answer" to this question becomes clear with one point: His command everywhere is one word that is faster than the blink of an eye, but the content of the command varies. If He commanded the fetus to complete its term in nine months, it will not be a moment more or less, and its "immediacy" lies in it being completed exactly at the end of this period. If He commanded the Earth to rotate once on its axis in 24 hours, His command is infallible. In other words, no time is required for His command to take effect; it is the content of the command that accepts time, considering the law of gradualness in the material world and the property and nature of motion.

4- The Beginning and End of Surah al-Qamar It is noteworthy that Surah al-Qamar begins with terror, anxiety, and a warning about the approach of the Resurrection, and ends with the absolute peace described for the true believers in the "seat of sincerity" in the presence of the "Sovereign, Perfect in Ability." Such is the method of education: it starts from anxiety and terror and ends in complete peace. it gathers scattered thoughts, tames rebellious desires, removes internal fear and anxiety caused by factors of annihilation, destruction, and error, and immerses one in peace, tranquility, and certainty in the proximity of the Lord's eternity and His mercy. Truly, the realization that Allah is the undisputed Owner and the unhindered Ruler throughout the universe, and the realization that He is All-Powerful and His power is effective in all things, grants man an unparalleled peace.

Some commentators have narrated that these two holy names (Malik and Muqtadir) have a profound effect on the answering of prayers. One narrator says: "I entered the mosque thinking it was morning, but it turned out much of the night remained and no one was in the mosque but me. Suddenly I heard a movement behind me and was afraid, but I saw a stranger calling out: 'O you whose heart is filled with fear, do not be terrified and say: Allahumma innaka Malikun Muqtadir, ma tasha’u min amrin yakun (O Allah, You are a Sovereign, Perfect in Ability; whatever You will of a matter comes to be), then ask whatever you wish.' He says: 'I recited this short prayer and asked nothing of Allah except that it was granted.'" [1]

O Allah! You are the Sovereign, the All-Powerful; grant us such success that, under the shadow of faith, action, and piety, we may be placed in the seat of sincerity, in the proximity of Your nearness and mercy. O Lord! We believe the Day of Resurrection is a terrifying day, bitter and unpleasant for the sinners; on that day, our only hope is in Your grace and generosity. O God! Grant us an awakened soul and an alert intellect so that we may take lessons from the fate of the predecessors and not tread the path that led to their destruction. End of Surah al-Qamar, 7 Rabi‘ al-Thani 1406.
